title: RGB: The Additive Color Model
concept: RGB represents color as three channels of light — red, green, and blue — that add together at different intensities to create every color a screen can show, from black (no light) to white (all three at full strength).

exercises.prompt: On a screen, how is a color like purple actually created?

options.text: By adding different amounts of red, green, and blue light together.
By mixing red, green, and blue paint on the pixel.
By using a single 'purple' light for every purple pixel.

exercises.explain: Right — screens don't store a color name, they add three light intensities together.

cards.title: Your screen has never used purple ink
cards.body: Look closely enough at any screen and you won't find purple, orange, or teal pixels — you'll find only tiny red, green, and blue lights at different brightnesses. Every color you've ever seen on a screen is those three lights, added together in different amounts.

cards.title: Red + Green + Blue = everything
cards.body: The RGB color model represents a color as three numbers — how much red light, how much green light, and how much blue light to add together. You already know each channel runs from 0 (off) to 255 (full brightness). RGB is called an additive model because you build colors by adding light, not by removing it.

cards.title: Three spotlights aimed at the same spot
cards.body: Picture a completely dark stage with three spotlights — one red, one green, one blue — all aimed at the same point. Turn up only the red one and the spot glows red. Turn up red and green together and the overlap glows yellow. Turn all three to full power and the spot turns white, because adding all the colors of light together gives you every wavelength at once.

cards.title: Building the secondary colors
cards.body: Pairs of primary lights add up to new colors: red + green makes yellow, green + blue makes cyan, and red + blue makes magenta. Push all three channels to 255 and you get white. Leave all three at 0 and you get black — the total absence of light.

cards.title: Light doesn't mix like paint
cards.body: A common assumption is that mixing more colors always makes a muddy brown, the way paint or crayons do. That's true for paint, which is subtractive — pigments absorb (subtract) light, so mixing more pigments removes more wavelengths and darkens the result. Light is the opposite: mixing more colors adds more wavelengths, which is why combining all three RGB channels gives you bright white, not brown.

cards.title: Two totally different ways to mix color
cards.body: Screens and paint solve 'make a new color' in opposite directions.

left.text:
Start at black.
Adding colors moves toward white.
Red + Green + Blue = white.

right.text:
Start at white paper.
Adding colors moves toward black.
Mixing pigments = darker, muddier.

exercises.prompt: A pixel is set to R=0, G=0, B=0. What does it look like?
exercises.explain: Correct — zero light in every channel means no light at all, which is black.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Turning all three RGB channels up to their maximum value produces white light.
exercises.explain: True — full red, full green, and full blue light added together appear as white to the eye.

exercises.sentence: RGB is called an ___ color model because colors are built by adding light together.
exercises.explain: Additive is right — you add red, green, and blue light to build up a color, ending at white.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of how a yellow pixel is displayed is accurate?

options.text: The red and green channels are both turned up high, with blue near zero, and the two lights blend into yellow.
The screen switches to a special yellow-only pixel just for that spot.
Yellow needs the blue channel turned up higher than red or green.

exercises.explain: Exactly — yellow on a screen is red light and green light overlapping, no yellow pixel required.

exercises.prompt: A designer wants a pixel to appear as bright cyan. Which RGB setting achieves that using the additive model?

options.text: R=0, G=255, B=255 — full green and blue light added together, no red.
R=255, G=255, B=255 — every channel at maximum.
R=0, G=0, B=0 — every channel off.
R=255, G=0, B=0 — only the red channel on.

exercises.explain: Right — cyan is the additive mix of green and blue light with red held at zero.
